schools.summary: Bethlehem of Judaea (the foretold town, distinct from Zebulun’s Bethlehem) in Herod’s days (d. 4 BC); the foreign Idumaean king signals Shiloh’s due time (Gen 49:10); the Magi learned Eastern students of the heavens.
Gentiles first seek the King (the firstfruits of Abraham’s spiritual posterity — Leo); the star a sign, not astrology (Gregory against the Priscillianists).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: the time fulfils Daniel’s seventy weeks; Chrysostom guards the star from the abuse of astrology.

themes: the wise men from the east

schools.summary: A widely diffused expectation of a ruler from Judaea (Tacitus, Suetonius); the star ‘at its rising’ (not followed eastward), perhaps Balaam’s ‘Star out of Jacob’ (Num 24:17); they come to worship the King.
Calvin and others: the Magi’s bold confidence in seeking the new-born King to adore him rebukes the indifference of Jerusalem.

themes: ‘Where is he… King of the Jews?’

schools.summary: Herod, the bloody Idumaean usurper (cf. Josephus), fears a rival; the dynastic dread that the kingdom return to native princes; all Jerusalem troubled from fear or worldliness.
Augustine: the Magi seek a Redeemer, Herod fears a successor; Leo/Gregory: the king of earth troubled at the King of heaven — earthly greatness confounded when heavenly shows itself.

themes: the false king and the true
trouble where there should be joy

schools.summary: Herod gathers the Sanhedrin — the chief priests (heads of the 24 courses, 1 Chr 24:4; Lk 1:5) and scribes (doctors of the law) — and demands where the Christ should be born (the prophetic present, Herod’s own words).
Chrysostom: the Jews rehearse the testimonies truly while Christ is out of sight; Calvin: the mention of a King awakens the forgotten predictions.

themes: the scribes who would not go

schools.summary: The scribes answer at once and without hesitation — Bethlehem, settled by prophecy (Mic 5:2); their sound knowledge yet deepens their guilt, pointing the Magi the way they would not go themselves.

The citation of Micah 5:2 gives the sense, not the letter: Bethlehem, ‘least’ in size, is ‘not the least’ in honour, for from her ‘shall come’ (born there — Chrysostom) the Governor who shall rule Israel.
Jerome explains the ‘little/not least’ sense; Gregory: fitting that the Bread of life be born in Bethlehem, ‘house of bread.’

themes: the Governor who shall rule Israel

schools.summary: Herod ‘privily’ (lathra) calls the Magi apart, concealing his aim from the Sanhedrin, to learn ‘accurately’ (akribōs) the time of the star — for the murderous calculation of the child’s age.
Gregory: he feigns a wish to worship only to discover and kill the child; the star had appeared nearly two years before (Pseudo-Augustine, Chrysostom).
Herod sends them the six miles to Bethlehem to ‘search exactly’ and report, feigning ‘that I may come and worship him also’ — ‘exquisite hypocrisy’ masking the design of murder.

themes: the trap that depended on their return

schools.summary: The star, hidden at Jerusalem, reappears and ‘went before them’ (proēgen) to the very place, standing over where the child was — a luminous appearance ministering to, not ruling, the Lord.
Ambrose: the star is the way, which is Christ — unseen where Herod is, seen again where Christ is; Remigius: the star God’s grace, lost by sin and found by repentance.
The heaped-up language (cognate accusative, ‘rejoiced with great joy exceedingly,’ Ps 67:4) marks the fulness of their gladness at the success of the long search.
The Gloss: true joy rejoices on God’s account; Pseudo-Chrysostom: they rejoiced that their hope was confirmed and their travel not in vain — the gladness of faith finding Christ.
They fall down in Eastern homage and open their ‘treasures’ (caskets) to present gifts — the customary approach to a king; their worship of a child in a mean condition a marvel of faith (Calvin).

Gregory/Augustine: gold for the King, frankincense for God, myrrh for the One who would die — owning Christ’s royalty, deity, and death; Chrysostom: Gentiles adoring God in the flesh (against Marcion, Paul of Samosata).
‘Warned of God’ (chrēmatisthentes, divinely instructed) in a dream not to return to Herod, they go home another way — thwarting the king’s trap and guarding the child.
Jerome: the warning came from the Lord himself, not an angel; Gregory: a parable — having found Christ, we return to our true country (Paradise) by another way, of humility, not the old way of sin.

themes: returning to God by a new way

schools.summary: The Magi gone, the angel again warns Joseph in a dream to ‘arise’ and flee to Egypt with the child and his mother till further word — ‘Herod will seek the young child to destroy him’ (Matthew omits the temple presentation, Lk 2:22).
Remigius: the angel always appears to Joseph in sleep; the wonder that the Son of God flees a tyrant — true humanity and deep humiliation, yet guarded by heaven.
Joseph rises at once and departs ‘by night’ for Egypt (a near refuge ‘replenished with Jews,’ outside Herod’s reach); the reverential phrasing ‘the young child and his mother’ befits the holy charge.
A short stay till Herod’s death; the citation (Hosea 11:1) refers directly to Israel’s Exodus, applied to Christ by type — ‘fulfilled’ in the NT sense of an event aptly answering the words.
Calvin: Hosea’s words point beyond the historical Exodus to the true Son in whom Israel’s sonship is fulfilled; Christ recapitulates the nation’s history.

themes: ‘Out of Egypt have I called my son’

schools.summary: Herod, believing himself deceived, in fury slays the male children of Bethlehem ‘two years old and under’ (by the star’s time, v.7); credible from his known character (though not in Josephus), the Child already safe in Egypt.
Augustine/Bede: the slain infants are the first martyrs, ‘an army clothed in white robes,’ figuring all Christ’s martyrs; Chrysostom: Herod’s insensate rage attempting the impossible.

The citation (Jer 31:15) follows neither Hebrew nor LXX (the Evangelists give the sense); ‘fulfilled’ in the accommodated sense — Jeremiah’s sorrow echoed in Bethlehem; Chrysostom: the prophecy soothes the horror, showing it foreseen.
Hilary: the children ‘were not’ only to earth — by martyrdom advanced to eternal life; Rabanus/Gloss: the refused comfort transfers all hope to the life to come.

Jer 31:15 originally described the captivity/destruction of Benjamin; Rachel (mother of Benjamin, buried near Bethlehem, Gen 35:19) figured weeping over her descendants — aptly applied to the slaughter.
Hilary/Rabanus: the children ‘are not’ only on earth but live in glory — and Jeremiah’s lament is set within a prophecy of hope (Jer 31:17, the children shall return).
Herod (the Great, distinct from the Passion-Herod) dies soon after, his dreadful end read as divine vengeance; the angel again appears to Joseph in a dream in Egypt to recall the family.
Calvin: Joseph’s persevering faith — firm in Egypt till recalled by God’s command; Henry: Egypt to sojourn in, not abide; Bede: a figure that all Christ’s persecutors perish.

The plural ‘they are dead’ (Herod alone named) may be plural-for-singular, or include Antipater and others; the command reverses the flight (v.13), echoing Exod 4:19 (Moses recalled) — Christ retracing Israel’s deliverer.
Joseph obeys exactly once more — arising, taking the child and his mother, returning to the land of Israel; the angel named the land but not the place, leaving him to further guidance.
Hilary: Joseph a figure of the apostles bearing Christ; Rabanus applies the return to the last times of the Jewish Church.

themes: return to the land of Israel

schools.summary: Archelaus, as cruel as his father (slaying 3,000 at a Passover), reigns in Judaea, so Joseph fears to go there; ‘warned of God in a dream,’ he turns to milder Galilee — prudence confirmed and directed by guidance.
No single text says ‘a Nazarene’; explained as Netzer/‘Branch’ (Isa 11:1), the Nazarite (Num 6:2), or one despised (Nazareth scorned, Jn 1:46); ‘the prophets’ (plural) = the collective sense of many.
Jerome/Pseudo-Chrysostom: Matthew appeals to the prophets collectively for a despised, rejected Messiah (cf. Isa 53:3; Ps 22:6).

debates.question: Which prophecy is meant by ‘He shall be called a Nazarene’?

sides.position: the general prophetic sense of a despised Messiah (Nazareth scorned)
